summary: Jeff Atwood explores his unexpected enthusiasm for the Microsoft Surface RT and the emerging category of touch laptops, arguing that touch interaction is becoming essential for all laptops. He compares the Surface RT with the Lenovo Yoga 13, finding that while the Yoga offers superior x86 performance, the Surface's design choices—fat screen with thin keyboard, small touchpad, and ARM efficiency—make it a better touch device. The post identifies key design challenges for touch laptops: screen bounce on thin hinged displays, oversized touchpads pushing screens too far away, widescreen awkwardness in portrait mode, practical size limits around 11 inches, and the critical importance of 8-10 hour battery life. Atwood concludes that ARM's power efficiency may eventually overcome the x86 compatibility advantage as chip performance improves dramatically.
key_points: Touch interaction complements keyboard use far more than expected, making touchscreens inevitable on all laptops
Intel x86 compatibility comes at a hefty cost: the Surface Pro is 44% larger and 33% heavier than the ARM-based Surface RT while offering shorter battery life
Traditional laptop design creates poor touch experiences—thin hinged screens bounce, large touchpads push screens away, and widescreen aspect ratios make portrait mode unusable
The 8-10 hour battery life threshold is psychologically significant: it eliminates charging anxiety and enables true all-day computing
ARM chip performance improvements (promising 75x gains by 2014) may eventually make x86 compatibility less critical as Intel struggles to match ARM's performance per watt
money_quotes: It is only a matter of time before all laptops must be touch laptops.
Intel inside comes at a hefty cost in weight, battery life, and size.
You need the inertia on the side you're touching.
A touchpad should be a method of last resort on a touch laptop anyway, because touch is more convenient.
There is truly something a little magical about that 10 hour battery life number.
I think we're beginning to uncover the edges of a world where lack of x86 compatibility is no longer the kiss of death it used to be.
In a land of ARM chips that pull an absolute maximum of 4 watts at peak, slapping Intel Inside will instantly double the size and weight of your device – or halve its battery life, your choice.
The Yoga 13 is just a nice laptop with a touchscreen slapped on it.
key_insight: The best touch laptops require fundamental design rethinking—not just adding a touchscreen to existing laptop form factors—with ARM's power efficiency potentially trumping x86 compatibility as touch becomes ubiquitous.
